turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some pretty bad stomach issues for a few weeks now. I get crampy lower abdominal pain, mostly on my left side, and I'm having bowel movements like 8 to 10 times a day, which is way more than usual - it's loose and watery with this urge to go all the time.

turns.student: Abdominal pain with bloody stools for three weeks — that's a significant combination. The left-sided crampy pain with blood and mucus suggests a colonic source. How many bowel movements are you having per day?
turns.patient: I'm having 8 to 10 bowel movements a day, like I said, and they're all loose to watery with urgency, but also sometimes I see bright red blood mixed with my stool - it's really frustrating and uncomfortable.

turns.student: Eight to ten times a day is a dramatic increase. With that frequency, are the symptoms disrupting your sleep? Do you wake up at night needing to go?
turns.patient: Yes, actually, I do wake up 2 to 3 times a night with this urgency to have a bowel movement - it's really affecting my rest and making me feel even more miserable. The fact that I'm waking up multiple times is just adding to my frustration.

turns.student: Nocturnal diarrhea is a red flag — it suggests an organic rather than functional cause. With that many bowel movements, do you feel like you're fully emptying each time, or is there a sensation of incomplete evacuation?
turns.patient: To be honest, after most bowel movements I get this uncomfortable feeling like I'm not quite done yet - it's really annoying and adds to my stress. This sensation of incomplete evacuation has been going on for most of these episodes, making me feel even more frustrated with how often I have to go.

turns.student: Tenesmus with bloody diarrhea points strongly toward an inflammatory process in the rectum and colon. With this level of GI inflammation, the body often responds systemically. Have you had any fevers or chills?
turns.patient: Yes, I've been experiencing a low-grade fever, around 37.8 to 38 degrees Celsius, a few times per week - it's not every day but it's definitely there when I'm having my worst episodes with diarrhea and cramping.

turns.student: Low-grade fevers fit the pattern. Inflammatory bowel disease often has extraintestinal manifestations — the same inflammatory process can affect joints. Have you noticed any joint pain or stiffness?
turns.patient: Yes, actually, I have been noticing some achy pain in both my knees and ankles, but it's not like I've had a big injury or anything - no swelling or redness, just this dull ache that's been bothering me for a while now.

turns.student: Knee and ankle pain — those are classic large joint manifestations of IBD. Are you taking anything for the joint pain? Any over-the-counter medications?
turns.patient: I've been self-medicating with loperamide, Imodium, to try and manage the diarrhea, but it's only given me minimal relief, if any at all. The joint pain is definitely more of a nuisance than anything else, but it's just one more thing on top of this whole ordeal that I'm trying to deal with.

turns.student: Ibuprofen is important to note — NSAIDs can actually worsen inflammatory bowel disease. That might be contributing to your flare. With bloody diarrhea eight to ten times a day for three weeks, you must be losing weight. Have you noticed any changes on the scale?
turns.patient: I've been trying to keep up with my work schedule and haven't really had time to check my weight, but between the fatigue from not sleeping well and the diarrhea, I feel like I'm burning calories just dealing with this stuff. To be honest, I haven't eaten much in the past few weeks because the thought of eating something and then having to deal with diarrhea afterwards is just overwhelming.

turns.student: Four kilos in three weeks is significant — that's consistent with the severity of your symptoms. You mentioned you work long hours at a startup. That kind of stress can trigger and worsen GI inflammation. How are you coping with all of this?
turns.patient: To be honest, I'm not really coping well. The constant diarrhea and pain have been taking a toll on me, both physically and mentally. I feel like I'm just trying to survive each day without losing my mind from the stress and discomfort. And to make matters worse, I've actually increased my drinking lately - I used to have 2-3 beers most weeknights, but over the past month or so, I've been having 4-5 beers per night because it seems like the only thing that helps me relax after a long day of dealing with this stuff.

turns.student: Increased alcohol use as a coping mechanism — I understand, but alcohol can also irritate the GI tract and worsen inflammation. Let me ask about family history — inflammatory bowel disease has a strong genetic component. Has anyone in your family had Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, or similar conditions?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30 years old - I've always been concerned that maybe there's a genetic link since we're close in age and everything. It's been on my mind lately with all these symptoms I'm experiencing, wondering if it could be something similar.

turns.student: Your sister having Crohn's is very significant. IBD runs in families, and the fact that she was diagnosed at 30 makes your presentation at 42 fit the pattern. Different family members can develop different forms — Crohn's versus ulcerative colitis.
turns.patient: That sounds about right to me. My sister and I are pretty close in age, so it's possible that we share some genetic factors that could be contributing to my symptoms. To be honest, I've been worried about this possibility for a while now, especially with all these bloody stools and the cramping - it's hard not to think about Crohn's or something similar when you know someone in your family has it.

turns.student: Let me review what you're taking. You mentioned loperamide with minimal effect, and the ibuprofen for joints. Any other medications or supplements?
turns.patient: I take ibuprofen 2-3 times a week just to help manage the joint pain - it seems like it's been worsening the abdominal symptoms, though. And that's about it, just those two things and I've been trying to stay hydrated with water and some electrolyte drinks to help with the diarrhea.

turns.student: The loperamide not working is expected — it's not effective against inflammatory diarrhea. And I'd strongly recommend stopping the ibuprofen given the likely diagnosis.
turns.patient: I was expecting that about the loperamide, but I'm a bit worried about stopping the ibuprofen completely since it helps with my joint pain. Could we discuss other options for managing the joint pain while we figure out what's going on with my gut?

turns.student: You mentioned you had an appendectomy at 16. Any other surgical history or hospitalizations?
turns.patient: No, just that one appendectomy when I was 16 - it went smoothly and I didn't have any complications afterward.

turns.student: Let me ask about your general health. Do you smoke or use any recreational drugs?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke, and I'm not into recreational drug use at all.

turns.student: Non-smoker, no drugs — that's good. Interestingly, ulcerative colitis is actually more common in non-smokers and ex-smokers.
turns.patient: That makes sense, I guess I've been lucky so far with not smoking or using recreational drugs. But what about my sister - does her having Crohn's make me more likely to have it too? And do you think that's what's going on with me, some kind of inflammatory bowel disease?

turns.student: You mentioned traveling to Mexico three months ago. Any GI issues during or after that trip?
turns.patient: No, I didn't have any GI issues while I was in Mexico for my work conference, and actually, I had no problems at all with diarrhea or cramping even when I returned from the trip - it wasn't until a few weeks later that these symptoms started showing up.

turns.student: No issues from the trip — that helps rule out a parasitic or infectious cause that might have been lingering. Any food allergies or medication allergies?
turns.patient: No, I don't have any known food allergies and no medication allergies that I'm aware of.

turns.student: Given the full picture — bloody diarrhea with mucus, left-sided crampy pain, nocturnal symptoms, tenesmus, weight loss, fever, extraintestinal joint involvement, family history of Crohn's, and NSAID exacerbation — this presentation is highly consistent with ulcerative colitis. We need to schedule a colonoscopy for definitive diagnosis.
turns.patient: I've been thinking that might be what it was - something like inflammatory bowel disease. The weight loss, the fever, the joint pain...it all seems to fit together in a way that makes sense. A colonoscopy sounds like the next step, I'm willing to do whatever it takes to get to the bottom of this and find some relief from these symptoms.

turns.student: Before we proceed with the workup, is there anything else you've been experiencing that we haven't discussed?
turns.patient: To be honest, I have been feeling pretty fatigued lately. It's not just the lack of sleep from waking up multiple times at night to use the bathroom, but also a general feeling of exhaustion throughout the day. The diarrhea and cramping are really taking a toll on my energy levels, making it hard for me to keep up with work and daily activities. I've been trying to push through, but it's getting harder to hide behind a mask of "tiredness" when in reality, I'm just feeling unwell overall.
